title: Logon Paths and Locking
text: Logon Paths and Locking Some DBMSs allow an application to open multiple logon paths. The DBMS maintains a state with each logon path as if every path were connected to a different application. To manage concurrent access, Uniface supports several different locking strategies and a version mechanism to improve locking performance. Multiple logon paths allow the application to execute multiple transactions independently. DBMS manufacturers generally use the term logon session , channel or connection instead of logon path . Most DBMSs also allow multiple applications (multiple logon paths) to execute concurrently. An application can obtain a lock on a record, ensuring that it cannot be changed or deleted by any other application or user of the same application, until the lock is released. Uniface Locking Strategies A Uniface locking strategy determines which action will trigger a lock request, but it does not determine the locking type in the DBMS itself. The connector does not need to be aware of the Uniface locking strategy being used. Uniface handles the implementation of locking strategies via the requests made to the connector. How you implement these requests in the DBMS is either up to you or the functionality offered by the API. Uniface supports three different locking strategies: Cautious locking in Uniface means locking a record as soon as a user makes the first modification to a database field in the entity. This is the default Uniface locking strategy. Cautious locking assumes that two users are likely to modify the same record at the same time. Optimistic locking in Uniface means delaying the lock until the moment that a record is actually written to the database. This can result in considerable performance improvements in multi-user environments. Optimistic locking assumes that two users will not modify the same record at the same time. Paranoid locking in Uniface means locking a record as soon as it is read. This can cause severe concurrency penalties if used in the wrong circumstances, but saves potential time wastage caused by other users simultaneously locking or modifying the same record. U_VERSION Uniface supports a version mechanism to improve locking performance. It is known by the name of the version field, U_VERSION. For the Delete and Update connector requests, records can be identified by the primary key or physical row address, and also by the U_VERSION field. Uniface automatically handles the logic of the U_VERSION mechanism. Uniface records the version of a retrieved record in U_VERSION. By comparing only the values of U_VERSION in the database and in the Uniface component, Uniface can see immediately during a write operation if the data in the database has been changed by someone else in the meantime (using the same or another Uniface application). Related Links Locking Strategies Locking means protecting one or more rows from being overwritten by other users while you are working with those rows. Working with rows can be defined as either the simple act of reading those rows from the database, or the act of modifying those rows after retrieving them and before storing, or the act of storing those modified rows. The state that initiates locking depends on the type of locking which has been applied or defined. U_VERSION for Improving Locking Performance Uniface supports a version mechanism which can improve performance by using a field called U_VERSION. U_VERSION is a one-byte field which you must assign to an entity for it to work. In other words, it is not automatically available. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
